entity: Arlington House, The Robert E. Lee Memorial
summary: I was working at Arlington House on the morning of September 11, 2001. We arrived early, preparing to open to the public, when news of the attacks came in. I remember watching the events unfold on a small television in Malcolm Willoughby's office with Colleen Curry and Linda Kacmarcik. After the Pentagon was hit, we evacuated the visitors and closed the house, with Keith Drews helping to shut down operations. The site implemented heightened security measures afterward, and we had to adjust our access routes since Fort Myer was closed.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, I first heard about the World Trade Center being hit while at Arlington House. A coworker, Keena Graham, called from home to alert us about the attacks. We then gathered in an office to watch the news coverage of the planes hitting the towers. The events in New York were the initial shock that set the tone for the rest of the day.

The Pentagon was hit shortly after the World Trade Center attacks on September 11, 2001. I was at Arlington House when we felt a shaking that we later realized was the plane hitting the Pentagon. We went outside and saw the smoke and heard sirens. Later, I volunteered at Arlington Hospital to help with Pentagon victims' families. The area around the Pentagon, including Fort Myer and nearby roads, was closed for days, affecting how we accessed work.

I experienced the September 11 attacks firsthand while working at Arlington House. The day started normally, but soon we learned of the planes hitting the World Trade Center and then the Pentagon. We evacuated visitors and closed the site amid growing chaos and uncertainty. The attacks led to new security protocols at Arlington House and restricted access through Fort Myer and nearby roads. The attacks deeply affected our operations and the surrounding community.

After the Pentagon was hit on September 11, 2001, I went to Arlington Hospital to volunteer, hoping to assist with victims' families. I usually volunteer on the Oncology Ward, and when I arrived, family members were coming in asking about their loved ones. The hospital was surprisingly calm and well-staffed, so they didn't need extra help at that time. Over the following days, we learned there were few survivors taken to hospitals from the Pentagon attack.

Fort Myer was a usual route for me to get to Arlington House, but after the Pentagon attack on September 11, 2001, it was immediately closed to the public. This closure forced us to find alternative routes, such as entering through the George Washington Parkway and the main cemetery entrance. The road closures around Fort Myer and the Pentagon lasted for days, complicating access to work.

Following the September 11 attacks, the George Washington Parkway became a critical route for me to access Arlington House. Since Fort Myer and nearby roads were closed, we had to enter through the main cemetery entrance via this parkway. For several days, this was one of the few ways to get in and out of work safely.

On September 11, 2001, after the Pentagon attack, Route 50 was one of the main roads available for leaving the area. I tried to use it to find alternate ways home, but many secondary roads were closed, funneling traffic onto major arteries like Route 50. It took me over an hour to travel about five miles home because of the congestion and closures.

After the Pentagon was hit on September 11, 2001, some of the alternative routes to leave the area near Arlington House involved going through Alexandria, Virginia. Because of road closures around the Pentagon and Fort Myer, traffic was heavily rerouted, and Alexandria became part of the detour routes for several weeks.

Malcolm Willoughby was a coworker at Arlington House who had a small television in his office. On the morning of September 11, 2001, Colleen Curry, Linda Kacmarcik, and I gathered in his office to watch the news coverage of the World Trade Center attacks. His office became an impromptu gathering spot as we tried to understand the unfolding events.

Colleen Curry was the curator at Arlington House on September 11, 2001. She was working on an inventory when the attacks began, and she joined me and others in Malcolm Willoughby's office to watch the news. After learning the Pentagon had been hit, I went to inform her and Mary Troy. Colleen left the site before we evacuated, but she was part of the early response that day.

Linda Kacmarcik was working with me at Arlington House on the morning of September 11, 2001. Along with Colleen Curry, we gathered in Malcolm Willoughby's office to watch the news coverage of the World Trade Center attacks. Linda was part of the small group trying to process the events as they unfolded.

Keena Graham was a coworker who happened to be off work on September 11, 2001. She called Arlington House from home to alert us about the attacks on the World Trade Center, which was the first notification we received. Her call prompted us to turn on the television and begin watching the events unfold.

Keith Drews was at Arlington House on September 11, 2001, and helped with closing down the house after the Pentagon was hit. He assisted in evacuating visitors and securing the site as we lost phone communications and had to make quick decisions. His role was important in managing the immediate response at the memorial.

Frank Cucurullo stayed with me at Arlington House for a while after the evacuation on September 11, 2001. We remained to assist with last-minute tasks to close the site properly before leaving. His presence was helpful during the chaotic hours following the Pentagon attack.

Mary Troy was helping with an inventory at Arlington House on September 11, 2001. After hearing about the Pentagon attack, I went to inform her and Colleen Curry. She was part of the staff involved in the early response to the unfolding crisis that day.

Joy Kinard was not mentioned in the context of the events on September 11, 2001, in my experience at Arlington House, so I don't have any direct memories or interactions involving her during that time.

On September 11, 2001, I noticed a strange plane flying unusually low and directly over Arlington House, which was not a typical flight path for National Airport. The plane was white or silver and didn't look like a commercial airliner. This sighting happened shortly after we learned the Pentagon had been hit, around 10:00 to 10:30 a.m., and it added to the surreal atmosphere of the day.

The United States Park Police played a significant role at Arlington House after the September 11 attacks. They increased their presence on the grounds as part of heightened security measures, especially after a suspicious incident involving a man making threatening comments about the Pentagon. Their vigilance helped maintain safety during a tense period following the attacks.
